title: Dog Lump at Vaccination Site: What to Do and When to Worry
meta_desc: Noticed a lump after your dog's shots? Learn why vaccinal granulomas happen, why they are usually low-risk, and how to monitor your pet safely.
content_html:
What is this lump?

It is very common for dogs to develop a small, firm, and painless swelling at the injection site shortly after receiving a vaccine. This is typically known as a vaccinal granuloma. It occurs because the body's immune system is reacting to the vaccine—which is exactly what we want it to do to build immunity. These lumps are usually localized inflammatory responses that involve white blood cells gathering at the site of the injection.

Is it an emergency?

The urgency level for a post-vaccination lump is Low. In the vast majority of cases, these bumps are harmless and will resolve on their own without medical intervention. However, you should monitor your dog for rare but serious allergic reactions, such as facial swelling, hives, or difficulty breathing, which would require immediate veterinary care. If the lump is small, non-painful, and your dog is acting normally, there is no need for an emergency visit.

Why a photo helps triage

Taking a clear photo of the lump next to a common object (like a coin) for scale is incredibly helpful for your veterinary team. By documenting the appearance and size of the bump on day one, you can compare it to new photos every few days. This visual record allows your vet to determine if the lump is shrinking as expected or if it is changing in a way that requires an in-person exam. If you notice the lump is growing after three weeks, or if it becomes red and painful, your photos will provide vital diagnostic history.

faqs.question: How long should a vaccination lump last?
faqs.answer: Most post-vaccination lumps will gradually disappear on their own within two to four weeks.

faqs.question: Should I massage the bump to make it go away?
faqs.answer: No, it is best to leave the area alone. Massaging or poking the lump can cause further irritation or discomfort for your dog.

faqs.question: When should I call my veterinarian?
faqs.answer: You should contact your vet if the lump persists for longer than three weeks, if it is larger than two centimeters, or if it appears to be growing rather than shrinking.
